title: Modular theory and affine representations on the Rindler horizon
abstract: We develop a group-theoretic interpretation of the Unruh effect based on affine symmetry on a light ray and relate it to modular theory. For a massless scalar field in two spacetime dimensions inertial and uniformly accelerated observers select two different flows within the same chiral one-particle structure, respectively, null translations and dilations. Minkowski modes are adapted to translations, while Rindler modes are adapted to dilations, with the Mellin transform providing the natural bridge between them. When a Minkowski positive-frequency mode is restricted to a single Rindler wedge, its comparison with Rindler modes is non-unitary within the positive-frequency sector. Modular theory gives the corresponding operator-algebraic interpretation: on the horizon the modular flow of the half-line algebra is implemented by dilations, and the restricted vacuum satisfies the KMS condition. The affine group thus appears as the minimal symmetry structure underlying thermality on the Rindler horizon.
summary: This paper provides a deep mathematical framework for understanding the Unruh effect by interpreting it through the lens of affine symmetry and modular theory. It shows that the thermal nature observed by an accelerated observer is mathematically equivalent to the modular flow of the restricted quantum field algebra. This establishes the affine group as the minimal symmetry governing horizon thermality.
main_problem: The paper develops a group-theoretic interpretation of the Unruh effect by relating it to modular theory and affine symmetry on the Rindler horizon.
main_result: The affine group structure captures the minimal symmetry underlying the Unruh effect, which is confirmed by modular theory showing the dilation flow implements the KMS condition on the restricted vacuum.
method: The analysis uses light-cone coordinates, compares Minkowski and Rindler mode expansions via the Mellin transform, and applies Tomita-Takesaki modular theory to characterize the thermal nature.
model_or_system: The system is a massless scalar field in two spacetime dimensions, analyzed by restricting its vacuum state to a single Rindler wedge near a horizon.
key_observables: The thermal factor $e^{-2\pi\omega}$ derived from the overlap between Minkowski and Rindler bases, and the satisfaction of the KMS condition.
important_parameters: The proper acceleration $\alpha$ (related to the temperature $T=1/(2\pi)$), and the spectral parameter $\omega$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the existence of a suitable vacuum state $|\Omegaangle$ and relies on the algebraic structure of the half-line algebra $A^+$.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical problem (Unruh effect) to developing the mathematical tools (affine symmetry, Mellin transform) and finally confirming the thermal nature using the rigorous framework of modular theory and the KMS condition.
why_interesting: The connection between the Unruh effect, which is fundamentally about thermalization in curved spacetime, and the rigorous mathematical machinery of modular theory provides deep insights into how entanglement and thermal properties emerge from local algebraic structures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Visualization of the algebra A+ and its v-translated U(γ)A+U †(γ) for positive γ. Notice that U(γ)A+U †(γ) ̸⊆A+ for γ < 0, since points are moved across the horizon (in this sense, the origin).
